name: Read-Write Lock

x.coordination: Allows many concurrent readers OR one exclusive writer. Readers share access; a writer excludes everyone. Fairness policy decides reader vs writer priority.
hazards: Writer starvation under a steady stream of readers (reader-preferring)
Higher overhead than a plain mutex
False sharing on the lock’s internal counters

def: A lock distinguishing shared (read) from exclusive (write) access, letting concurrent readers proceed together while serialising writers.
problem: A plain mutex serialises read-mostly data needlessly; readers do not conflict with one another.
mechanism: Tracks reader count and writer state; multiple readers acquire shared mode simultaneously, while a writer needs exclusive mode and waits for readers to drain. A fairness policy (reader- or writer-preferring) prevents one side from starving the other.
benefits: Higher read throughput for read-mostly data
limits: More overhead than a mutex (worse if writes are common)
failures: Deadlock when a reader tries to upgrade to a writer
Using it where reads are not actually dominant
whenNot: Write-heavy or balanced workloads (a mutex is simpler/faster)
Very short critical sections (mutex overhead is lower)
myths: "RW locks are always faster for reads" — under writes or contention they often lose to a plain mutex or RCU
